title: Control Flow
description: Master Java's decision-making and loop structures — the conditional and iterative constructs that determine how your program flows. CSE110 exams test control flow through tracing exercises, error spotting, and predicting exact output. This chapter covers every form: if/else chains, switch (classic and expression), ternary, for, while, and do-while — with special focus on the subtle behaviors that distinguish correct code from buggy code.

sections.content: The `if` statement is the backbone of decision-making. Java evaluates conditions strictly as `boolean` expressions — unlike C/C++, **you cannot use an integer as a condition**. Every `if` condition must produce `true` or `false`.

sections.title: if/else Forms — Complete Reference

sections.label: = vs == in Conditions
sections.content: Unlike C/C++, `if (x = 5)` is a **compile-time error** in Java — you cannot use assignment as a condition since `int` is not `boolean`. However, `if (b = true)` compiles and runs (it assigns `true` to `b`), which is a logical trap. Always use `==` for comparison.

The ternary operator `condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse` is a **single expression** that evaluates to one of two values. It is commonly tested in CSE110 output prediction questions because its evaluation order surprises students.

sections.title: Ternary Operator — All Cases

sections.label: Ternary is an Expression, not a Statement
sections.content: A ternary produces a **value**. Both branches must be **type-compatible** — you can't mix `String` and `int` as the two options. A ternary can be used wherever a value is expected: in assignments, as arguments to methods, or in print statements.

The `switch` statement tests one expression against multiple constant values. The most important behavior to understand for CSE110 is **fall-through**: without `break`, execution continues into the next case.

sections.title: switch — Classic Form with Fall-Through Analysis

sections.label: What switch Can Accept
sections.content: In Java, `switch` works with: `byte`, `short`, `int`, `char`, `String` (Java 7+), and `enum`. It does **NOT** work with `long`, `float`, `double`, or `boolean`. Each `case` value must be a **compile-time constant** — you cannot use a variable as a case value.

sections.title: switch Expression (Java 14+ — modern form)

sections.content: The `for` loop is Java's most structured iteration construct. Its three-part header — `initialization; condition; update` — all execute in a well-defined order. **Tracing this order** is the key to predicting `for` loop output.

sections.title: for Loop — All Forms

sections.content: Use `while` when you don't know in advance how many iterations are needed — the condition is checked **before** each iteration. Use `do-while` when the body must run **at least once** regardless of the condition.

sections.title: while and do-while — Side-by-Side

sections.label: Key Difference: while vs do-while
sections.content: The difference matters when the condition might be **false from the very start**. `while(false)` — body never runs. `do{...}while(false)` — body runs exactly once. In CSE110 exams, questions often hinge on this distinction with tricky initial values.

`break` exits the **innermost** loop or switch. `continue` skips to the **next iteration** of the innermost loop. Java's **labeled break/continue** targets an outer loop — this is the Java answer to goto for nested loop exits.

sections.title: break, continue, and Labeled break
Exercise 1: if-else Chain Tracing
sections.explanation: First block: `x=15`. `15>20` false, `15>10` **true** → prints B. The chain stops (only one branch executes). Second block: `15>10` true → E. Third: `15>5` true → F. Fourth: `15>20` false → nothing. Note: the second/third/fourth are separate `if` statements (not `else if`), so ALL are evaluated independently.

sections.title: Exercise 2: switch Fall-Through Trap
sections.explanation: `n=2` matches case 2. Prints 'Two'. No `break` → falls through to case 3, prints 'Three'. The `break` after case 3 exits the switch. Cases 4 and default are not reached. Key: fall-through starts from the **matching** case and continues until a `break` or end of switch.

sections.title: Exercise 3: for Loop with break and continue
sections.explanation: Loop: i=1 (odd, not >7) sum+=1→1. i=2 (even) continue. i=3 (odd) sum+=3→4. i=4 continue. i=5 (odd) sum+=5→9. i=6 continue. i=7 (odd) sum+=7→16. i=8 (8>7) break. Final sum=16.

sections.title: Exercise 4: while vs do-while with Same Condition
sections.explanation: `while(5<5)` is false immediately → body never runs. Prints `after-while`. `do...while`: body runs FIRST (prints D5, b becomes 6), then checks `6<5` which is false → exits. Prints `after-do`. This is the fundamental difference: while checks condition first, do-while checks it last.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Labeled break in Nested Loops (Tricky!)
sections.explanation: Trace step by step. i=1: j=1(1*1=1, not >6, count=1), j=2(1*2=2, count=2), j=3(1*3=3, count=3), j=4(1*4=4, count=4) — inner loop completes fully. i=2: j=1(2*1=2, count=5), j=2(2*2=4, count=6), j=3(2*3=6, 6>6? NO, count=7), j=4(2*4=8, 8>6? YES → `break outer` exits BOTH loops). Final count = 7. The labeled break stops immediately without incrementing count again.

sections.title: Exercise 6: The Dangling else Trap
sections.error: Logical Error (misunderstood indentation). The `else` pairs with the **nearest unmatched if** — which is `if (x > 20)`, NOT `if (x > 10)`. So when x=15: outer if is true, inner if (15>20) is false → the else prints 'Greater than 20'? No: the else runs when inner if fails. Output is actually `10 or less`. But the programmer's intent was probably `10 or less` to print when x≤10.
sections.explanation: This is the classic **dangling else** problem. Java (like most languages) resolves it with the rule: an `else` always pairs with the **nearest preceding unmatched if**. Without braces, the indentation is misleading. The else pairs with `if(x>20)`, not `if(x>10)`. For x=15: outer if (true) → inner if (false) → else runs → prints '10 or less'. **The golden rule: always use braces.** Ambiguous else is a real exam trap.

items: **Dangling else**: `else` always pairs with the nearest preceding unmatched `if`. Use braces to clarify.
**switch fall-through**: without `break`, execution falls into the next case. Usually a bug, sometimes intentional.
**`break`** exits innermost loop/switch. **Labeled `break`** exits the named outer loop.
**`continue`** skips current iteration. **Labeled `continue`** skips to the next iteration of the named outer loop.
**do-while semicolon**: `} while (condition);` — the semicolon at the end is **required** and easy to forget.
